content:
I wasn’t building a moat. I was draining one. Each gain in my speed was a reduction in the difficulty of being me — and that difficulty was the only thing that made me hard to replace.

5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Dmytro Glazunov on UnsplashI made myself twice as fast with AI. I want to be honest that I did this eagerly, with the specific pride of someone who thinks they’re getting ahead. I learned the tools early, I built the workflows, I became the person who could turn around in a day what used to take a week. And I made myself, in the exact same motion, about half as necessary. Those weren’t two things that happened to me. They were one thing, and I did it to myself, and I’d probably do it again, which is the part worth explaining.
The logic felt airtight at the time. Be more productive, be more valuable, be more secure. It’s the oldest career advice there is and it isn’t wrong, historically. For most of the history of work, making yourself faster made you more useful and more useful made you safer, because the bottleneck was always how much a person could do, and a person who could do more was a person worth keeping. I was running a strategy that had worked for everyone who came before me. I just didn’t notice the strategy had quietly stopped working, because the thing that made it work — the scarcity of capacity — was the exact thing I was destroying.
Here’s the mechanism, as plainly as I can put it. My value to the company was never really my output. It was my output relative to how hard that output was to obtain elsewhere. I was valuable because doing what I did was hard, and doing it well was rare, and the company couldn’t easily get it another way. Every hour I spent making myself faster with the tools, I was making my own output easier to obtain — not just for me, for anyone, because the tools that doubled my speed are the same tools available to my replacement, to a cheaper hire, to the colleague two desks over, to the company itself. I wasn’t building a moat. I was draining one. Each gain in my speed was a reduction in the difficulty of being me, and the difficulty of being me was the only thing that made me hard to replace.
So I got twice as fast, and in getting twice as fast I taught the organization that the work could be done twice as fast, which means it now needs half as many hours of it, which means — if nothing else changes — it needs half as many people like me. I didn’t just improve my productivity. I lowered the market value of my own role, and I did it visibly, in front of the people who decide how many of my role to keep.
There’s a darker fold in this, which is that the very act of using the tools well is a form of training my replacement. Every workflow I built, every prompt I refined, every clever way I found to get the machine to do the hard part — all of it is documentation. It’s a map of how to do my job without my judgment, drawn by my judgment. The better I am at making the tools do the work, the more completely I’ve specified how the work can be done without me. I’m not being paranoid; I’m describing what a workflow is. A workflow is the part of your expertise you’ve made transferable. I spent a year making my expertise transferable, and transferable is a synonym for “no longer dependent on you specifically.”
I want to resist the obvious wrong conclusion, which is “so don’t use the tools, stay slow, stay precious.” That’s not available, and it’s not even right. The colleague who refuses to get faster doesn’t preserve their scarcity; they just become the slow person in a world that now moves fast, which is its own kind of replaceable, the worst kind. You can’t opt out of the productivity gain — everyone around you takes it, and standing still while they speed up just makes you the obvious cut. The trap is real and there’s no exit by going backward. Both roads — get faster, stay slow — lead to less necessary. That’s what makes it a trap and not a mistake.
If there’s an exit, and I’m genuinely not sure there is, it’s sideways, and it has to do with what kind of fast you make yourself. Getting faster at the part of the job the tools can also do is the move that erases you, because you’re racing the tools at their own game and the tools don’t get tired or get raises. The only speed that doesn’t reduce your scarcity is speed at the part the tools can’t do without you — the judgment about which of the machine’s answers is right, the taste, the knowing-what’s-worth-doing, the relationships, the read on a situation that isn’t in any data. If I’d spent the year getting twice as fast at that, I’d have made myself more necessary, because that’s the part that doesn’t become transferable just because I documented my workflow. But that’s not what the tools make easy to improve. The tools make it easy to improve the legible, automatable middle of the job, which is exactly the part that, once improved, doesn’t need you. They optimize the wrong half, the half whose improvement is also your obsolescence.
So I sit with this now, twice as fast and half as necessary, having run a strategy that used to be wisdom straight into a wall that’s new enough that nobody warned me. I don’t think I was foolish. I think I was using a map that had just gone out of date, the way you’d drive confidently toward a bridge that was there last year. The advice to make yourself more productive assumed a world where capacity was the bottleneck. The tools removed that bottleneck, and removed it for everyone at once, and in a world where capacity is cheap, the person who optimized for capacity optimized for the thing that just became worthless.
I’d tell anyone reading this who feels the same pride I felt — the pride of being the fast one, the early adopter, the one who turns it around in a day — to do one uncomfortable piece of arithmetic. Ask whether the thing you’re getting faster at is the thing the machine can also do, or the thing it can’t. If it’s the former, every gain is a small withdrawal from your own necessity, and the feeling of getting ahead is the feeling of sawing the branch you’re sitting on, very efficiently, with a tool you’re proud to have mastered. I know the feeling well. I mistook it for progress for an entire year. It was progress. It just wasn’t mine.
